subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and wellâfocused research question with consistent thematic focus throughout the investigation
Appropriate and varied secondary methods with informed source selection visible
Accurate and effective communication of topic, temporal scope and sector in introduction and conclusion
weaknesses: Methodology lacks explicit criteria for source selection and detailed triangulation procedures
Conclusion summaries stop short of critically tying evidence back to the research question
Minor repetition in wording of research question suggests slight lapses in precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission excels in communicating a precise topic and research question (band-6 performance) and demonstrates a reasonable variety of secondary methods, but it falls short of the highest band by omitting explicit sourceâselection criteria and detailed triangulation (band-4). Aggregating these strands yields a solid mid-band performance meriting 4/6.

strengths: Generally relevant use of industryâstandard sources (EY Brexit Tracker, FCA, TheCityUK) to support argument
Clear application of specialist terminology and economic concepts in most sections
weaknesses: Numerous missing citations for key statistics and duplicated bibliography entries
Instances of misdrawn diagrams (PPC curve shift) and imprecise phrasing that undermine consistency
Assertions (GDP decline, balanceâofâpayments impact) often lack empirical backing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the essay demonstrates clear knowledge of economic models and applies source material in places, frequent citation gaps, diagram inaccuracies and unsupported claims place performance at the lower end of the 3-4 band. A score of 3/6 reflects mostly relevant but inconsistently applied knowledge.

strengths: Logical use of quantitative data with economic models to draw causal links between Brexit and firm relocation
Reasoned discussion of regulatory interventions and their implications for Londonâs competitiveness
weaknesses: Analysis often descriptive rather than deeply critical; counter-arguments (e.g. Covid-19, fintech) are noted but underexplored
Limited critical appraisal of source methodologies (GFCI, EY Tracker) and superficial evaluation of data limitations
Discussion of strategic drivers behind firm responses lacks depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation delivers clear, supported analysis with coherent arguments, yet stops short of consistently well-supported, critically evaluative thinking (it resides in the 4-6 band). Persistent descriptive passages and underdeveloped counterfactual discussion limit the depth, justifying a mid-band mark of 4/12.

strengths: Well-structured overall essay following IB conventions with logical headings, numbered figures and comprehensive sections
Title page, abstract, contents and bibliography are correctly included
weaknesses: Layout issues: uneven margins, inconsistent heading styles, misaligned table-of-contents leaders
Citation formatting inconsistent, figure captions duplicating alt-text and missing access dates
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure fully meets the highest band, but repeated layout and formatting inconsistencies place the work in the middle band (1-2 for layout and 3-4 for structure averaged to 3/4). The score of 3/4 reflects strong organization but partial application of formatting conventions.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly quoted in bold on the title page and re-stated in the introduction and conclusion.  It is narrow (relocation of investment banking operations) and time-bounded (2019-2024), giving the essay a clear dependent variable (scale of relocation) and independent variable (Brexit).  All subsequent analysisâjob-loss figures, asset transfers, passporting regulationâlinks directly back to this focus, satisfying the requirement for a clearly stated, well-focused and effectively connected question.

The student states the topic, temporal scope (2019â2024) and sector (investment-banking relocations) in the opening lines and reiterates them in the research question, introduction and conclusion.  Each section returns to the stated focus, avoiding unrelated Brexit dimensions such as fisheries or immigration.  The chosen lens (London versus EU hubs) is unquestionably suitable for systematic economic investigation and is consistently communicated through diagrams and quantitative indicators.  Minor wording overlaps (e.g. two versions of the research question) do not materially detract from clarity, so the strand aligns with the top-band descriptor.

Most evidence (EY Brexit Tracker, TheCityUK statistics, HSBC survey) is clearly linked to the argument and integrated into diagrams and narrative.  Nonetheless, several secondary claims are drawn from generic, non-academic sites (Investopedia, blogs) or lack precise citations, and some duplication appears in the bibliography.  Overall application is mostlyâthough not consistentlyârelevant and appropriate, corresponding to the 3-4 band.

The essay follows the prescribed EE structure with a title page, abstract, contents, introduction, methodology, main body, conclusion and bibliography.  Headings and sub-headings are logical and guide the reader effectively; diagrams and tables are numbered and referenced in-text.  Minor lapses (duplicate section numbering, slight inconsistency in heading styles) do not impede overall clarity, so the work fulfils the highest presentation band for structure.

Basic layout conventionsâpage numbers, title, word count, and figure captionsâare in place, but several issues persist: uneven margins, inconsistent underline weight, misaligned table-of-contents leaders, and some image captions duplicating alt-text.  Citation formatting shows repetition and missing access dates.  These shortcomings indicate that layout considerations are applied only partially, corresponding to the 1-2 band rather than the fully correct 3-4 band.

The student offers a reasoned discussion of Londonâs diminished EU share versus global resilience and evaluates government interventions.  Limitations of data and methodology are acknowledged in the conclusion, showing some critical reflection.  Nevertheless, evaluation often remains descriptive, and alternative perspectives (e.g., the role of Covid-19, long-run structural change) are only briefly addressed.  The argument is coherent but not deeply critical, situating the work at the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The analysis interprets quantitative evidence (asset totals, employment figures) with economic models to establish causal links between Brexit, regulatory change and firm relocation.  Comparative and ADâAS diagrams are used to justify claimed effects on Londonâs GDP and competitiveness.  However, several numerical claims are left without methodological context, and counter-arguments (pandemic, fintech growth) are noted but not fully explored.  The depth is therefore âclearâ but not yet âconsistently effectiveâ, fitting the 4-6 band.

The essay demonstrates sound knowledge of the post-Brexit regulatory environment (passporting, MiFID II) and of Londonâs historical advantages.  Key economic modelsâADâAS, S-D with barriers, PPCâare applied correctly, indicating clear conceptual understanding.  However, some assertions (e.g., 1.5 % fall in London GDP) are unreferenced, and the discussion of comparative advantage is simplified.  These inconsistencies keep the work within, but at the top of, the 3-4 band.

The student draws on a sizable body of data: job-relocation tallies, asset-transfer estimates, FDI values, and GFCI rankings.  Most sources are topical and drawn from reputable industry trackers, regulators or financial media, demonstrating clear relevance to the question.  Yet, the dependence on secondary commentary, limited primary documentation, and occasional citation gaps indicate that research, while largely appropriate, is not consistently rigorous, meriting a mid-band (4-6) placement.

The methodology outlines comparative, regulatory and case-study approaches and justifies reliance on secondary data while acknowledging limitations.  A reasonable range of sources (EY Tracker, FCA blogs, bank reports, Statista, FT articles) is named, showing informed selection.  However, criteria for including specific datasets are not spelled out, triangulation procedures remain vague, and no primary data collection is attempted.  These gaps place the work in the upper-middle band rather than the highest.

Specialist terms such as âpassportingâ, âFDIâ, âMiFID IIâ, âeuro-clearingâ, and âcomparative advantageâ are used frequently and generally accurately.  Diagrams are labelled with correct economic terminology.  Minor lapsesâoccasional imprecise phrasing ("monetary advisory companies") and a misdrawn shift direction on PPC 2âprevent full consistency, so the performance aligns with a solid but not flawless 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The statistic that 44% of firms planned relocations is presented without a footnote; add a precise citation to EY Brexit Tracker.
The literature review is mostly descriptive; including critical appraisal of source relevance, methodology and bias would strengthen evaluation.
Discussion of staff relocations lacks analysis of why firms varied in response; exploring strategic drivers would deepen insight.
Labels for PPC curves in text and diagram are unclear; explicitly state which curve corresponds to firms that expanded into the EU versus those that remained.
The claim on euro-derivative market share requires a precise citation and discussion of source methodology to validate its accuracy.
Quantitative changes like GDP impacts and unemployment shifts should be contextualized with counterfactual scenarios or comparative benchmarks.
The 1.5% decline in Londonâs GDP needs a footnote citing the data source to bolster credibility of economic impact claims.
The narrative introduces the ADâAS model but does not explicitly reference Diagram 3 in the text; link figures clearly to the discussion.
Image 1 is presented without sufficient analysis of regional shifts; interpret key differences in growth intentions rather than just displaying data.
Reliance on GFCI data would benefit from a critique of its methodology and the indicators used to rank financial centres.
Key statistics such as referendum vote shares are presented without citation; add precise in-text references to support these assertions.
The discussion of economies of scale would be strengthened by referencing long-run average cost curves and how dual-region operations affect them.
The overview of FCA interventions lists changes but does not evaluate their effectiveness; include examples or metrics showing impact on firm behaviour.
The claim that relocations bolster the UK balance of payments is asserted without supporting data; include empirical evidence or references.
The essay lacks a dedicated reflection section on decision-making and planning; including this would address the engagement strand.
The conclusion summarizes findings but stops short of critically answering the research question with specific evidence; tighten focus on the stated inquiry.
The GFCI rankings table is not interpreted in the narrative; discuss how shifts in rank affect Londonâs relative competitiveness.
Diagram no. 4 axes are labeled but the text does not fully connect the supply shift to documented regulatory cost increases; make this link explicit.
This assessment paragraph covers multiple jurisdictions and policies; separating analysis of Paris, Frankfurt and Dublin would clarify each interventionâs effect.
Economic notation is implemented with an HTML <math> tag; use LaTeX notation (e.g. $$Y_e$$) for consistency with academic standards.
The statistic on business globalisation intentions is cited but not linked back to the financial sector focus; clarify its relevance to banking relocations.
The supply-barrier shift is misdrawn to the right; increased regulatory costs should shift supply inward (leftward) in the model.
The alt-text and image description repeat in detail rather than focusing on interpretive analysis; streamline descriptions and link directly to argument.
The inclusion of an accurate word count on the title page meets IB formatting requirements.
The title clearly communicates the investigationâs focus on Brexitâs impact on Londonâs financial sector, establishing a precise purpose and lens for systematic inquiry.
The research question is stated twice with slightly differing wording; consolidating to a single, consistent formulation will strengthen coherence.
Table of contents leaders are misaligned and dot-leader formatting is inconsistent; aligning entries visually will improve navigability.
The research question is restated here with slightly different phrasing; ensuring uniform wording throughout will improve presentation consistency.
Methodology lacks explicit criteria for source selection and details of triangulation procedures; specify these to demonstrate rigor.
EMIR is mentioned but its mechanism and impact on clearing operations is unexplained; provide conceptual context to demonstrate understanding.
Identical entries for the EU single market source appear twice in the footnotes; remove duplicates to avoid redundancy.
The reflection on topic choice is largely descriptive; deeper analytical insight into how this interest shaped methodology would enhance engagement.
The Pre-Brexit background is presented as one long paragraph; breaking it into thematic subsections would aid reader clarity.
Citation style here uses an informal parenthetical note rather than a numbered footnote; convert to consistent IB citation style.
